history: The client has a history of recurrent dreams during graduate school about being unprepared for a thesis defense, suggesting longstanding anxiety about meeting expectations. Their father is described as having high expectations, which may have contributed to internalized pressure to achieve. The client frequently oscillates between feeling competent and fearing inadequacy, as seen in their reflections on learning and teaching mathematics. They report both proactive engagement with intellectual challenges and frustration when their preferred learning style is at odds with external demands.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I receive criticism, it means I have failed to meet important standards, and others will see me as less competent. To avoid feeling inadequate, I must always be fully prepared and avoid mistakes. My worth is tied to my ability to perform without error, especially in the eyes of authority figures.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't meet expectations or avoid criticism, it means I'm fundamentally inadequate and will never be good enough, no matter how hard I try. This makes me feel hopeless about ever achieving a sense of competence or acceptance.
coping_strategies: The client immerses themselves in intellectual activities and over-prepares to manage feelings of inadequacy. They seek mastery through proactive learning and teaching, sometimes using visual-spatial strategies to cope with cognitive overload. When faced with criticism or perceived failure, they may withdraw or ruminate on their shortcomings.
situation: Receiving criticism during a work performance review.
auto_thought: I'm not good enough; they're going to think I can't handle this job.
behavior: I become quiet and avoid eye contact during the review. Afterward, I replay the criticism in my mind and start planning how to over-prepare for future tasks.
